Case Study

How do you compress AI prompts without losing the instructions that matter?

Tokko is a prompt compression tool I designed and built from scratch. Web app, Chrome extension, public API. Shipped it, got real users, posted on Reddit, learned the core economics were wrong. Built the whole thing in about 4 weeks.

Role

Product Designer

Timeline

~4 weeks

The problem

People who use AI tools like Claude and ChatGPT keep running out of messages or paying more than they need to. Most prompts are full of filler. Politeness, redundancy, filler phrases that add tokens but don't change the AI's response. The question: what if you could strip the filler before sending it? Same response, fewer tokens.

Simple idea. But the execution surfaced a design problem I didn't expect: compression breaks things. It strips filler, yes. But it also strips instructions that matter. Format rules, URLs, things the AI was told not to do. A shorter prompt that loses meaning is worse than a verbose one.

That became the real design challenge. Not "make prompts shorter" but "make prompts shorter without breaking them."

What I shipped

Before getting into the design decisions, here's the scope of what I built:

Prompt Compressor

Two panel optimizer. Paste a prompt on the left, get a compressed version on the right. Three compression modes: Balanced, Aggressive, Smart.

Constraint Integrity

System that detects URLs, format rules, and prohibitions before compression, then verifies they survived. Re inserts anything that was stripped.

Chrome Extension

Floating compress button inside Claude.ai. Compresses prompts without leaving the chat interface.

Playground

Side by side comparison tool. Sends both original and compressed prompts to Claude, shows both responses. Users prove compression works themselves.

Key design decisions

Why a two panel layout (and how I got there)

The optimizer didn't start as two panels. I sketched several layouts before landing on the final design. The breakthrough was Google Translate. Input on the left, output on the right. You type, it transforms, you copy. That mental model was already familiar to people. I didn't need to teach anyone how the tool works.

But Tokko isn't translation. It's compression. So the output panel needed to feel visibly smaller. Shorter text, fewer lines. The difference had to be visual, not just numerical. That gives users immediate confidence that something actually happened.

Constraint integrity: the hardest design problem in the product

This wasn't on any roadmap. I found it by testing my own product. I was compressing prompts that had specific instructions: format rules, URLs, things the AI was told not to do. After compression, some were gone. The prompt was shorter but it was also wrong.

I tested dozens of prompts and found three categories of constraints that compression would strip:

URLs

Links got stripped, shortened, or mangled

docs.api.com/v2/ref → removed

Format rules

"Respond in JSON" or "use bullet points" disappeared

respond only in JSON → gone

Prohibitions

"Do not mention X" was removed entirely

do not mention competitors → gone

The solution: before compression runs, the system scans for constraints and flags them. After compression, it verifies each one survived. If something was stripped, the system re inserts it. No extra API calls. Pure pattern matching.

The hardest design problem was communicating "something went wrong, but we fixed it" without making users lose trust in the compression itself. The solution was to frame it positively. "3 constraints protected" instead of "3 constraints were lost." Same information, completely different emotional response.

Every state, not just the happy path

Most side projects only design the success state. I designed every state a user could hit: empty, loading, success, error. Across the optimizer, analytics, history, settings, and global error boundaries. 15+ specific error states total. This is what separates a prototype from something a developer can actually build.

Proving it works instead of claiming it

Instead of telling users "trust me, compression preserves quality," I built a tool where they can prove it themselves. The Playground compresses a prompt, sends both the original and compressed versions to Claude, and shows both responses side by side. You can literally see that the output is the same. Show, don't tell.

What the market said

I posted Tokko on Reddit and community forums. The reception taught me more than building did.

The feedback that hit hardest challenged the core assumption: for one off prompts, you spend tokens to save tokens. The math doesn't work. The only scenario where the economics make sense is system prompts that get reused thousands of times. Compress once, save forever. That's a much narrower audience than "everyone who uses AI."

I didn't get defensive. I replied honestly: "You're right. For one off prompts, you're spending tokens to save tokens. That doesn't make sense and I won't pretend it does." That reply got more engagement than any feature announcement I made.

Honesty builds more trust than polish. Facing the economics problem early would have changed everything. But shipping first and learning from real feedback still got me to the right answer, just slower.

Impact and reflection

I shipped a complete product: web app with auth, database, analytics, Chrome extension, public API, and developer docs. Got real users and real feedback. The core value prop turned out to be wrong for the mass market, but the design work, especially constraint integrity, solved a genuine problem.

Found the real problem by using my own product

Constraint integrity wasn't planned. It came from testing, noticing prompts breaking, and deciding that a shorter but broken prompt is worse than no compression at all.

Designed for trust, not just function

The Playground exists because "it works" isn't convincing. Letting users prove it themselves is. That's a design decision, not a feature decision.

Validated (and invalidated) with real users

Shipped, posted publicly, engaged with criticism honestly. Learned the economics were wrong. That's more valuable than a polished deck that never ships.

Next move: validate before building

If I continue, I'd pivot from "make prompts shorter" to "make prompts better." If I start something new, I start with the feedback loop first. Landing page, waitlist, conversations. Then build.

I shipped a real product, got honest market feedback, and came out of it with a clear understanding of what I'd do differently. That's not a failure. That's how products work.
